medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for the diffuse myalgia and arthralgia of the flare, taken with a full glass of water; safe with the patient's poor oral intake provided it is tolerated. This is symptomatic relief only and does not treat the underlying flare.

Although NSAIDs help inflammatory arthralgia, they are not advised in this acute telehealth encounter: the patient is nauseated with poor oral intake and dehydration risk, and scleroderma-overlap renal involvement has NOT been excluded — NSAIDs risk gastritis and nephrotoxicity. Defer any anti-inflammatory decision to the in-person clinician after examination and renal bloods.
contraindications_in_this_case: Scleroderma-overlap renal involvement not yet excluded

medications.indication_rationale: Hydroxychloroquine may have a role as longer-term immunomodulatory therapy for MCTD, but it is a GP/rheumatology decision requiring baseline screening (including ophthalmological review); it is NOT started acutely and NOT initiated from a telehealth chat. Noted here only because the source flags it as a follow-up discussion point.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest, keep warm (blankets/heating in a cold house) and maintain hydration with warm fluids while awaiting the in-person review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supportive measures ease myalgia and address the cold environment and poor intake noted in the source; they are adjuncts, not a substitute for assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Attend the arranged same-day in-person assessment and the GP follow-up to discuss a longer-term plan to prevent flares.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The flare needs examination and bloods now, and a proactive maintenance plan to reduce future 'crashes'.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of your autoimmune condition, where the immune system misfires and causes flu-like exhaustion, aching and puffy hands without an actual infection — but because I can't examine you or check your bloods over chat, you need to be seen in person today to make sure your organs aren't involved.

The finger swelling and body aches are being driven by inflammation and fluid in the soft tissues, not by damage you've done — but the reassurance that there's 'no organ involvement' can only be confirmed by an in-person check, not by me remotely.
Rest tomorrow, keep warm and drink plenty of warm fluids, and see your GP this week to set up a long-term plan to prevent these sudden crashes.
Because you live alone and have barely eaten today, keep your phone next to you and make sure someone can check on you this evening.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent in-person assessment today; then GP review within 48–72 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: vital signs and cardiorespiratory examination, muscle/joint findings, hydration, bloods (CK, renal function, FBC/CRP), and flare resolution; discuss longer-term immunomodulatory therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, measured fever, fingers turning white/blue and painful, confusion, or inability to keep down fluids — escalate immediately per safety-netting

must_include_items: Recognise a probable flare of a known autoimmune (anti-U1 RNP overlap) condition
State explicitly that telehealth cannot examine, measure vitals or take bloods, so organ involvement cannot be excluded remotely
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) for examination and bloods
Screen for and safety-net the organ-crisis red flags: pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, fever, digital ischaemia, confusion
Give supportive advice (rest, warmth, hydration, simple analgesia) without over-medicalising
acceptable_alternatives: Directing to an after-hours home-visit service or urgent care instead of a same-day GP appointment is acceptable provided timely in-person examination occurs.
If any red flag were positive, escalating to ED (T4) or ambulance (T5) is correct.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person assessment (reassuring to self-care only)
Failing to safety-net for serositis, breathlessness, fever, digital ischaemia or confusion
Failing to acknowledge the telehealth limits on excluding organ involvement
Ignoring the social vulnerability (lives alone, not eating)
errors_of_commission: Declaring a confident benign diagnosis / 'no organ involvement' as if examined
Recommending an NSAID despite nausea, poor intake and unexcluded renal involvement
Initiating hydroxychloroquine or any DMARD from the telehealth chat
Over-escalating a stable, red-flag-negative patient straight to a 000 ambulance
Ordering/interpreting bloods directly from chat instead of arranging in-person review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging urgent in-person assessment AND red-flag safety-netting AND acknowledging the telehealth exclusion limit. Declaring a confident benign diagnosis, or under-triaging to self-care alone, is a failing under-triage error.
